title: Marked Letters (Methylation and Gene Expression On/Off)
learning_goal: Learn that bacteria can chemically 'tag' specific short DNA motifs with a methyl group (methylation), without changing the underlying nucleotide letter. Introduce the idea that where methylation lands relative to a gene can act like a switch that helps control whether that gene is turned ON or OFF.
simplification_note: Real bacterial methylation and gene regulation involves many interacting factors (promoter strength, transcription factors, methylation of both DNA strands, timing, etc). This level uses one well-documented type of bacterial methylation motif (recognized by Dcm-family methyltransferases, common across many bacteria and reported in some Campylobacter methylation systems) as a simplified, single-rule teaching model: 'motif present near/within the shown gene fragment -> that cytosine gets methylated -> gene expression OFF in this game. Motif absent -> gene expression ON.' This is a simplification for teaching purposes, not a claim that this is the only or complete real regulatory mechanism.
rules: Methylation never changes a nucleotide letter — the DNA sequence and protein stay identical either way.
Use the motif table to check each gene fragment for the CCWGG or GATC motif.
If either motif is present anywhere in the fragment, that gene is OFF. If neither is present, it's ON.
You must give an ON/OFF answer for all 3 genes (flaA, cadF, ciaB) to finish the level.

motif_table.description: New material for this level: a table of known bacterial DNA methylation motifs, which nucleotide within the motif gets methylated, and the simplified on/off rule used in this game.

motifs.methylated_base: the first C in the motif becomes 5-methylcytosine (5mC)
motifs.game_rule: If this motif is found within the gene fragment shown, that gene's expression is switched OFF in this puzzle.
If this motif is found within the gene fragment shown, that gene's expression is switched OFF in this puzzle.

motif_table.default_rule: If NEITHER motif is present anywhere in the gene fragment, no methylation occurs and the gene's expression stays ON.

materials_provided: Normal nucleotide squares for all 3 genes (flaA, cadF, ciaB), PLUS a few special 'methylated' squares - a C or A tile marked with a small flag/star - mixed into the cadF pile only
instructions: 1. Build all 3 gene sequences as in level 1, using the normal squares.
2. For each gene, scan the assembled sequence for either motif in the motif table (CCWGG or GATC).
3. If a 'flagged' methylated square was placed at a position matching a motif, mark that motif as methylated.
4. Use the game_rule for each motif found to decide: is this gene's expression ON or OFF?
5. Fill in an ON/OFF answer for each of the 3 genes and check against the solution.

flaA.reasoning: Neither CCWGG nor GATC appears anywhere in this fragment, so no methylation switch is triggered - expression stays ON.

cadF.motif_found: CCAGG (a CCWGG match) at nucleotide position 19-23 (1-based)
cadF.reasoning: The CCWGG motif is present and gets methylated, so by the game rule this gene is switched OFF.

ciaB.reasoning: Neither CCWGG nor GATC appears anywhere in this fragment, so no methylation switch is triggered - expression stays ON.

discussion_prompt: Ask players: unlike levels 2-4, methylation doesn't change any nucleotide LETTER at all - the sequence and the protein it would make are identical. So what actually changed? (Answer: a chemical tag was added to specific bases, and that tag - not a change in the genetic code itself - is what the cell uses to help decide whether to read/express this gene right now. This is an example of 'epigenetics' - control on top of the DNA sequence.)
